title: Handling Q&A When You Don't Know the Answer
description: What should you say when someone asks a question and you do not know the answer? Learn calm Q&A phrases for meetings, presentations, and interviews while you practice English vocabulary.
text:
In 2018, former PepsiCo CEO Indra Nooyi told students at Yale that leaders are judged not only by the answers they give, but also by how they handle questions they cannot answer immediately. In many workplaces, Q&A is part of meetings, presentations, job interviews, and client calls. The problem is simple: sometimes you do not know the answer. The skill is not to pretend. The skill is to respond clearly, protect trust, and keep the conversation useful.

A common mistake is to answer too fast. Research on conversational pressure shows why. In 2014, social psychologist Alison Wood Brooks at Harvard Business School published work on anxiety and performance, showing that pressure can reduce clear thinking. In Q&A, that pressure pushes people to fill silence with weak guesses. A better first move is to buy a little time. Short phrases do this well: “That is a useful question.” “Let me think about the best way to answer that.” “I want to be accurate here.” These sentences are simple, but they change the pace. They show respect for the question and give your brain a few extra seconds.

The next decision depends on why you do not know the answer. Sometimes you understand the topic but need a moment to organize your reply. In that case, you can bridge from the question to what you do know. Communication trainers often call this bridging. A practical phrase is, “I do not have the final number in front of me, but I can explain the process we used.” Another is, “I cannot confirm that detail yet, but here is what we know so far.” This keeps the answer honest while still giving value.

Sometimes the question is too broad, too hostile, or slightly off the main topic. Then the goal is redirection, not avoidance. You acknowledge the question and move it to a useful frame. For example: “There are two parts to that. Let me take the first one first.” Or: “That issue is important, but to stay with today’s decision, the key point is our timeline.” In media training and executive communication, this method is used to stop a discussion from becoming confused. The questioner still feels heard, but the group returns to the purpose of the meeting.

There are also moments when the only correct answer is that you do not know. Saying this directly can sound risky, but false certainty is riskier. A strong phrase is, “I do not know the answer to that yet.” The important part comes next: “I will check with the data team and send you a response by 3 p.m. tomorrow.” Follow-up works because it turns uncertainty into a task with an owner and a deadline. In project management, trust often depends less on immediate knowledge than on reliable closure.

Confidence in these moments comes mostly from structure. Executive coach Matt Abrahams of Stanford Graduate School of Business teaches speakers to use clear verbal patterns under pressure. One useful pattern is acknowledge, answer what you can, and commit to next steps. For example: “Good question. I cannot verify the cost figure right now. What I can say is that the budget increased after the supplier change, and I will confirm the exact amount this afternoon.” This sounds calm because the speaker is not fighting the gap in knowledge.

The last step is the follow-up itself. If you promised an email, send it. If you promised a document, attach it. In client work and internal teams, people remember the missing answer less than they remember whether you returned with the correct one. That is why a short, accurate reply after the meeting often repairs the moment completely: “Following up on your question from this morning, the renewal rate was 82 percent in Q2, based on the CRM report updated on July 5.”

cardDescription: Learn practical phrases for buying time, redirecting difficult questions, promising follow-up, and staying confident in professional Q&A.
